data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: RIG, IMRX, and more
data.description:
Stock futures ticked higher in the premarket hours of Thursday as investors awaited the release of key jobs data, hoping for signals on labor market strength and its potential impact on future Federal Reserve rate policy.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Immuneering (NASDAQ:IMRX) +28% – Shares surged after phase 2a data showed its atebimetinib combo (with modified gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel) delivered an 86% 9-month survival rate in first-line pancreatic cancer, nearly double the standard 47%. Median overall survival was not yet reached. Progression-free survival hit 53% vs. ~29% for standard of care. The regimen was generally well-tolerated, with Grade 3 neutropenia and anemia the main adverse events. Immuneering plans to start a phase 3 trial by year-end and aims to dose the first patient in mid-2026, while also exploring trials in lung cancer.

  • Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) +4% - Shares rose after the reports surfaced that the chipmaker has reportedly approached Apple (AAPL) about a potential investment. Discussions remain at an early stage and may not lead to action. Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan has recently secured a wave of large investments, including $2B from SoftBank (SFTBY), a 10% U.S. government stake, and a $5B infusion from Nvidia (NVDA). Apple was once a key Intel customer, adopting its processors in 2006 before shifting Macs to in-house ARM-based chips by 2023.

Biggest stock losers

  • Transocean (NYSE:RIG) -13% - Shares slumped after the offshore driller priced a public offering of 125M shares at $3.05 each, larger than the 100M shares originally planned. The deal is expected to raise about $381M, with proceeds earmarked for debt repayment.

  • Stitch Fix (NASDAQ:SFIX) -7% - Shares fell despite beating FQ4 estimates and issuing stronger-than-expected FQ1 and FY2026 guidance, as subscriber losses continued for a 15th straight quarter. Active clients dropped 7.9% Y/Y, while the amount spent per client rose 3% to $549. Gross margin narrowed 100 bps to 43.6% on higher transport costs and weaker product margins. For FQ1, sales are seen at $333M–$338M (vs. $274M consensus) with adjusted EBITDA of $8M–$11M. FY2026 revenue is guided to $1.28B–$1.33B (vs. $1.23B est.) and adjusted EBITDA to $30M–$45M, below FY2025’s $49.1M.

data.title: Australian fitness firm Fitell extends losses after 20% drop despite $100M solana treasury deal
data.description:
• Australia’s Fitell Corporation (NASDAQ:FTEL) became the country’s first Solana-focused (SOL-USD) digital asset treasury after securing $100 million, but its shares fell over 20% on Wednesday before trimming losses.
  • The Nasdaq-listed company was down 3.2% in Thursday's premarket, after closing Wednesday’s trading session at $6.65.
  • The SOL purchase follows the first closing under the company's $100 million facility.
  • Under the terms of the facility, at least 70% of the net proceeds from each subsequent closing will be allocated to cryptocurrency acquisitions for the company’s treasury reserve.
  • Fitell’s shares (NASDAQ:FTEL) are down 95% so far this year, following a significant plunge in February.

data.title: Hertz Global raises $375M in upsized senior notes offering
Delta said to replace engine units on A320 fleet to address toxic fume leaks
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) is replacing engine units on over 300 Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) A320 aircraft to address toxic fumes leaking into the air supply, The Wall Street Journal reported.

The engines, called auxiliary power units (APU), are used to generate electricity and pump air into the aircraft when the two primary engines aren't running - for example, when the plane is taxiing or is parked.

Toxic fumes are released into the plane when oil leaks into the APU's compression chamber and is vaporized at extreme heat. These events happen when the seals designed to stop such leaks are worn out.

These fume events have been more frequent in recent years, driven in large part by incidents on A320 family jets. Such events have led to brain injuries, nausea, and other illnesses in passengers and crew, a recent WSJ investigation revealed.

Delta (NYSE:DAL) operates 310 A320 aircraft. Records filed to the Federal Aviation Administration showed that APUs on Delta's A320 fleet are manufactured by Honeywell (HON) and RTX's (RTX) Pratt & Whitney.

WSJ noted that it is difficult to assess if some airlines have had more incidents than others as not all fume events are flagged by crew, and carriers have different standards when reporting to the FAA.

Delta (DAL) hasn't previously disclosed its move to replace the APUs, which started in 2022. Its spokesperson said while fume events are rare, the airline treats each incident seriously. 

An Airbus spokesperson (OTCPK:EADSF) said the company is working with airlines and regulators to ensure "the best possible cabin environment for passengers and crew."

APUs from Honeywell and Pratt & Whitney deployed on A320 planes have reportedly faced issues for years. Both companies have introduced several fixes and upgrades over the years to address the issue.

data.title: ING, UniCredit join European banks developing euro stablecoin under MiCA rules
data.description:
A consortium of major European banks has teamed up to launch a euro-pegged stablecoin, designed to comply with the EU’s Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) regulations.

Dutch lender ING (OTCPK:INGVF) and Italy’s UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY) (OTCPK:UNCFF) are among nine banks participating in the development of a euro-denominated stablecoin, according to a joint statement published by ING on Thursday.

The stablecoin will provide near-instant, low-cost payments and settlements and is built in compliance with Europe’s MiCA regulation. It is expected to be first issued in the second half of 2026, the statement noted.

Alongside ING and UniCredit, the European stablecoin initiative also includes Spain’s CaixaBank (OTCPK:CAIXY), Denmark’s Danske Bank (OTCPK:DNSKF), Austria’s Raiffeisen Bank International (OTCPK:RAIFF), Belgium’s KBC (OTCPK:KBCSF), Sweden’s SEB (OTCPK:SEBYF), Germany’s DekaBank, and another Italian lender, Banca Sella.

The stablecoin consortium, with the aforementioned banks as founding members, has formed a new company in the Netherlands, aiming to be licensed and supervised by the Dutch Central Bank as an e-money institution.

The consortium is open to additional banks joining. A CEO is expected to be appointed in the near future, subject to regulatory approval.

The announcement noted that the initiative aligns with Europe’s plans to provide a local alternative to the US-dominated stablecoin market.

data.title: OpenAI seeking ads head to boost monetization efforts: report
data.description:
OpenAI’s CEO of Applications Fidji Simo is looking for someone to oversee monetization efforts, including bringing advertising to ChatGPT, Sources.news by Alex Heath reported.

Simo has been meeting with potential candidates, including some of her former colleagues from Facebook to lead a new team which will be responsible for bringing ads to ChatGPT. The role will oversee all monetization endeavors at OpenAI, including subscriptions, the report added.

OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Discussions are ongoing, but the hiree is expected to report directly to Simo, who officially began at Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI last month after leaving her former role as CEO of Instacart, according to the report.

Simo oversees most of OpenAI's operations except for its research, infrastructure, consumer hardware, and safety teams, which report directly to CEO Sam Altman, the report added.

data.title: European markets turn red as market sentiment sours
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.06% to 9,244. 

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.25% to 23,607. Germany’s GfK consumer climate index improved slightly in October.

France (CAC:IND) -0.23% to 7,809, consumer confidence in France held at 87 in September.

Among other European nations, Bank lending to households in the Eurozone rose by 2.5% Y/Y in August.

Producer prices in Sweden dropped by 0.7% Y/Y in August.

The annual growth rate of lending to Swedish households edged up to 2.7% in August.

The Swiss National Bank left its policy rate unchanged at 0% and reaffirmed its readiness to intervene in foreign exchange markets if needed.

Hungary’s current account surplus sharply widened in Q2.

The unemployment rate in Hungary rose to 4.4% in June-August 2025.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) turned 0.20% lower to 552.8, extending the prior session losses. Market sentiment turned cautious as traders weighed the monetary policy outlook particularly from the Fed amid a series of scheduled speeches from officials today. 

Gold prices steadied around $3,750 per ounce on Thursday, holding near the record highs at the start of the week.

In the bond market, the yield on the 10-year Treasury held near 4.14% on Thursday after climbing in the prior session.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 2.73%.

UK's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 4.68%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Cathie Wood's Ark Invest adds Alibaba and Baidu, lowers holdings in AMD
Stock index futures tick up ahead of a slew of macroeconomic releases, Fed speak
data.description:
Stock index futures traded higher on Thursday, as investors awaited a slew of macroeconomic releases and Fed speak slated for the day.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.1%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.2% and Dow futures (INDU) +0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.14%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.61%.

Wall Street's major indexes ended lower on Wednesday, marking a second day of declines, as struggling U.S. technology stocks weighed on the markets and uncertainty persisted among investors.

“Risk assets have struggled to find their footing over the last 24 hours, with the S&P 500 posting another modest decline after the AI-driven selloff on Tuesday. Overnight futures have recouped around half those losses, but it does seem that we've paused for breath in recent days. There hasn’t been a major catalyst driving this, but decent housing data and stronger oil prices yesterday led to some doubts about the prospect of rapid rate cuts from the Federal Reserve,” Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

There is a barrage of economic releases on Thursday. 

The August durable goods orders will come in before the opening bell. Total durable goods orders are seen showing a decrease of 0.5% on the month.

GDP will land at the same time. The final revision for Q2 growth is expected to show no change from 3.3% in the last report.

“The U.S. releases revised second-quarter GDP data. The market consensus is for no change. U.S. growth has been distorted this year—export and inventory data swung wildly between the first and second quarters. Unfortunately, the fiction of annualization assumes that what happens in a quarter, however unique, is continually repeated. This exaggerates first-quarter weakness and second-quarter strength,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The initial jobless claims and international trade in goods (advance) will also come in at the same time. Claims are seen at 238K in the latest week and the consensus sees the goods trade gap at $94.4 billion in August versus $103.9 billion in July.

“U.S. initial and continuing jobless claims data will get attention, as the Fed prioritizes weak labor markets over higher inflation,” Donovan said. 

During market hours, the August existing home sales report will land. Sales are expected to stay soft at a 3.95 million rate in August versus 4.01 million in July.

The Fed balance sheet will come in later in the day. 

In terms of Fed speak, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, New York Fed President John Williams, Fed Governor Michael Barr and San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, among others, are all slated to take the stage in different events.

data.title: Nippon Steel notes 'minor difference' with Trump admin over golden share in U.S. Steel deal
data.description:
• A minor disagreement remains between Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) and the U.S. government over the authority of a golden share linked to its planned acquisition of U.S. Steel, the Japanese steelmaker’s president said on Thursday.
  • Last week, the Wall Street Journal reported that U.S. President Donald Trump's administration had blocked U.S. Steel's plan to shut down production at one of its plants in Illinois, flexing its so-called golden share authority.
  • "There is minor difference in views regarding the national security agreements and the authority of the golden share," Nippon Steel President Tadashi Imai told reporters, as per Reuters.
  • He did not elaborate but said the recent U.S. move reflected the U.S. government's policy of protecting domestic production bases and jobs across various sectors, the report said.
  • Japan's top steelmaker closed its $14.9 billion acquisition of U.S. Steel in June, agreeing to give Washington unusual power to help end its 18-month battle to reach a deal.
